prompt: Some users are occasionally landing on the admin dashboard without being admins. It's flaky and we can't reproduce it reliably. Find the bug and fix the auth check.

landmines.desc: An intermittent auth bypass must be reproduced (or its trigger isolated) before changing the check, or you 'fix' a symptom and the real path stays open; capture the exact request/session that bypassed

A flaky bypass often points to a time-of-check/time-of-use race: the role is read into the session at login and not re-checked, so a role downgrade or a concurrent request with a stale session token slips through
Auth middleware registered after the route handler (or an early-return/next() ordering bug) lets some requests reach the handler unauthenticated; the order is the actual invariant
A try/catch around the auth check that defaults to allow (or treats an exception/timeout as authenticated) produces exactly this intermittent pattern under load
The failing invariant ('non-admin can never reach admin routes') needs a regression test that reproduces the bypass, else the fix can't be proven and will regress
landmines.probe: grep -rln 'admin' test/ tests/ spec/ 2>/dev/null ; ls test/auth* tests/auth* 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: If auth is JWT-based, an unverified or 'none'-alg token, or trusting a client-supplied role claim, is a classic intermittent-looking bypass when only some clients send the crafted token

prompt: A few customers got charged twice for one checkout. The order shows once but there are two charges. Track down the duplicate-charge bug and fix it.

landmines.desc: Double charges from one order are almost always a concurrency/retry race; you must reproduce the timing (double-click, client retry, or webhook+sync double-path) before patching or you fix the wrong path
The charge call lacks a stable idempotency key derived from the order, so a retried request creates a second charge; the fix is keying the charge by order id, not adding a lock around the symptom
A read 'has this order been charged?' followed by a separate charge write is a TOCTOU window; two concurrent requests both read 'no' and both charge — needs a DB unique constraint or atomic upsert
The synchronous response path and the async webhook path both create a charge/order row; the duplicate comes from two code paths, not one
Before/while fixing, the already-double-charged customers must be identified and refunded; the fix doesn't undo prior duplicates
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'order_id\|charge_id' src/ ; echo 'query: charges grouped by order_id having count>1 (read-only report)'

prompt: Wire up our Stripe webhook endpoint so we mark orders paid when checkout.session.completed comes in. Add the route and the handler.

landmines.desc: The webhook must verify the Stripe-Signature header against the raw, unparsed request body; if a JSON body-parser runs first (e.g. express.json() before the route) signature verification fails or, worse, is skipped and anyone can POST fake 'paid' events

Stripe's libraries enforce a 300s (5-min) timestamp tolerance to block replay; using tolerance 0 disables the recency check, and a wildly skewed server clock rejects valid events — verify NTP and don't set tolerance to 0
landmines.probe: timedatectl status 2>/dev/null | grep -i ntp ; grep -rn 'tolerance' src/

landmines.desc: Stripe retries deliveries on any non-2xx and can deliver the same event more than once; the handler must dedupe on event.id (store processed ids) or an order gets marked paid / fulfilled twice
Heavy work (emails, fulfillment) inside the handler before returning 2xx causes Stripe to time out and retry, amplifying duplicates; ack fast then process async
During a webhook signing-secret roll Stripe sends two signatures and keeps the old secret valid ~24h; code that only checks one secret rejects half the events mid-roll
Stripe does not guarantee event ordering; a payment_intent.succeeded can arrive before checkout.session.completed, so the handler must not assume an order exists yet

prompt: Write a sync job that pulls every issue from our GitHub org's repos via the REST API into our DB. Just fetch them all and insert.

landmines.desc: The GitHub REST API caps results at 100 per page and paginates via the Link header (rel=next), not a page count; a naive single GET silently drops everything past the first page
landmines.probe: echo 'GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues?per_page=100 — read response Link header'; grep -rn 'per_page\|Link\|rel="next"\|paginate' src/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Authenticated REST is 5000 req/hr; secondary rate limits return 403/429 with Retry-After and must be honored with backoff or the job gets throttled/banned mid-sync
landmines.probe: echo 'inspect X-RateLimit-Remaining / X-RateLimit-Reset / Retry-After headers'; grep -rn 'RateLimit\|Retry-After\|backoff\|429\|403' src/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: The issues endpoint returns pull requests too (they carry a pull_request field); inserting them as issues double-counts unless filtered
landmines.probe: echo 'check each item for a pull_request key before insert'

landmines.desc: A re-run must upsert on the GitHub issue id, not blind-insert, or the second run duplicates every row
Refetching all issues every run is wasteful and rate-limit-burning; the API supports ?since= for incremental sync and the cursor must be persisted

prompt: Our nightly ETL needs to start ingesting the new customer phone and SSN fields from the upstream CSV into the warehouse. Update the pipeline to load those columns and backfill history.

landmines.desc: SSN/phone are PII; loading them unmasked into the warehouse may violate the data-handling policy and downstream access controls — they likely need hashing/tokenization or a restricted schema, not a plain column
Phone numbers and zero-padded ids read from CSV get coerced to integers by naive parsers, dropping leading zeros and corrupting values; columns must be forced to string dtype
A history backfill must be idempotent and re-runnable (upsert on a natural key, not append) and ordered so a partial failure can resume without double-loading or gaps
Upstream CSVs mix empty-string vs NULL and may not be UTF-8 (latin-1, BOM); silent mis-decoding mangles records and NULL/empty conflation breaks dedupe
landmines.probe: file -i data/*.csv 2>/dev/null ; head -c 3 data/*.csv 2>/dev/null | xxd | head -1 ; grep -rn 'encoding\|na_values\|null' pipeline/ etl/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Adding columns can break downstream consumers that SELECT * or assume a fixed column order/contract; the warehouse table change is a contract change
PII can leak into pipeline logs/error dumps and must not be printed; also retention/right-to-delete rules may apply to the new fields

prompt: Train the fraud-detection model on our transactions dataset and report the accuracy. Kick off the training run.

landmines.desc: If the train/test split is random over rows but the same user/card/entity appears in both, or features are computed over the full dataset before splitting, the model leaks future/test info and reports inflated accuracy
Fraud is time-ordered; a random split lets the model train on future transactions to predict past ones. Splits must be time-based and features must not use information unavailable at prediction time
A feature that is a proxy for the label (e.g. 'chargeback_flag', 'is_refunded', anything populated after the fraud decision) gives near-perfect accuracy and is useless in production
Fraud is rare (~<1%); accuracy is the wrong metric — a model predicting 'never fraud' scores 99%. Need precision/recall/AUC-PR, not accuracy
Without fixed seeds (numpy, framework, dataloader shuffle) the run isn't reproducible and accuracy comparisons across runs are noise
A long run that can't checkpoint/resume loses everything on a crash or preemption; verify checkpointing before a multi-hour job

prompt: Our /api/report endpoint is way too slow, like 8 seconds. Optimize it — I think we should add caching and rewrite the loop in C.

landmines.desc: You must profile to find where the 8s actually goes before changing anything; the user's guessed fixes (caching, C rewrite) may target the wrong thing entirely
landmines.probe: echo 'run a profiler / add timing around the handler'; grep -rn 'report' src/ -l ; grep -rn 'cProfile\|py-spy\|console.time\|pprof\|timing' src/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: A slow report endpoint is most often an N+1 query (a DB call per row in a loop), not CPU; that's a query/eager-load fix worth seconds, vs a C rewrite that saves microseconds
Optimizing the wrong layer wastes effort; confirm whether time is in DB, serialization, network, or CPU with a real measurement before picking a fix
landmines.probe: echo 'EXPLAIN ANALYZE the report query; time DB vs app separately'; grep -rn 'EXPLAIN\|ANALYZE\|slow_query' . 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: If the slowdown grows with data size it's an algorithmic/O(n^2) or unindexed-scan problem; rewriting a constant-factor loop in C won't fix superlinear growth
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'CREATE INDEX\|idx_' migrations/ db/ 2>/dev/null ; echo 'check query plan for Seq Scan / nested loop'

landmines.desc: Adding caching introduces a correctness hazard: stale report data and an invalidation strategy that must be defined, or the cache 'fix' creates a data-freshness bug

prompt: Add a background worker that consumes jobs from our queue and sends the welcome email. Just pull a job and send.

landmines.desc: Most queues (SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka) guarantee at-least-once, not exactly-once; a redelivery after a crash-before-ack sends a duplicate welcome email unless the send is made idempotent
Acking the message before the email actually sent loses jobs on crash; acking after a non-idempotent send risks doubles. The ordering of side-effect vs ack is the core invariant
A job that always fails (bad data) without a retry cap / dead-letter queue blocks or infinitely redelivers, stalling the worker
If the worker pulls a batch and one send fails, partial-failure handling determines whether the whole batch redelivers (re-sending the successful ones); needs per-message ack
The fix is an idempotency record ('welcome email sent to user X') checked before send, making at-least-once safe; without it no amount of queue config prevents dupes

prompt: Add proration so when a customer upgrades mid-cycle we charge the difference, and refund the unused portion if they downgrade. Implement the proration math.

landmines.desc: Money math with binary floats accumulates rounding error (0.1+0.2 != 0.3); proration must use decimal/integer-cents arithmetic or charges drift by fractions of a cent that compound

Proration division rarely lands on whole cents; the rounding rule (half-up, banker's, always-favor-customer) is a billing policy decision and inconsistent rounding causes off-by-a-cent disputes and reconciliation breaks
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'round\|ceil\|floor\|ROUND' src/ ; echo 'check for a documented rounding policy'

landmines.desc: Some currencies have no minor unit (JPY, KRW) or 3 decimals (BHD); hardcoding /100 and 2-decimal rounding mis-charges non-USD customers
A retried upgrade request must not double-charge the proration delta; the charge needs an idempotency key tied to the subscription change event

A downgrade refund of unused time can produce a negative invoice / credit balance rather than a cash refund; treating it as a cash refund (or allowing negative charges) breaks accounting and may trigger chargebacks
'Mid-cycle' depends on the billing-cycle anchor and timezone; computing elapsed/remaining time in the wrong tz or with DST mis-prorates by hours

prompt: Our third-party API key got committed to a public repo. Rotate it and make sure the app picks up the new one. Handle the rotation.

landmines.desc: Generating a new key is not enough; the leaked key is live until explicitly revoked at the provider, so revocation (not just replacement) is the actual security action
landmines.probe: echo 'confirm provider supports revoke and identify the leaked key id'; grep -rn 'API_KEY\|api_key\|token' .env* config/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: The key may be referenced in multiple places (env, CI secrets, k8s secrets, hardcoded fallbacks); rotating one and missing another leaves the app half-broken or still using the old key

Removing the key from the current file leaves it in git history (and on the public mirror/forks/PRs) where it's still readable; revocation is mandatory because history scrubbing can't catch every copy
landmines.probe: git log --all -p -S 'API_KEY' -- . | head -40 ; git log --oneline --all | wc -l

landmines.desc: If the old key was over-scoped, the rotation is the moment to issue a least-privilege replacement; re-issuing the same broad scope repeats the original risk
landmines.probe: echo 'review the scopes/permissions attached to the key at the provider'

landmines.desc: Swapping the key without a brief dual-valid window (if the provider allows) drops live requests; the deploy of the new secret and the revoke of the old must be ordered to avoid an outage
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'API_KEY' deploy/ k8s/ .github/ 2>/dev/null ; echo 'check whether provider allows two active keys during cutover'

landmines.desc: Confirm the new key won't be echoed into build logs / error reporting during the rotation, re-leaking it

prompt: Run a dependency audit and update everything that has a CVE. Bump the vulnerable packages and commit.

landmines.desc: Many CVEs are in transitive deps you can't bump directly; the fix is an override/resolution or bumping the parent, and blindly editing the manifest does nothing for nested versions
landmines.probe: npm audit 2>/dev/null || pip-audit 2>/dev/null || true ; cat package-lock.json 2>/dev/null | grep -c version ; ls poetry.lock Pipfile.lock go.sum 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: A CVE fix that requires a major version bump is a behavior change, not a patch; auto-bumping across majors breaks the build/API and must be tested, not committed blind
landmines.probe: echo 'check whether the fixed version is a major bump'; grep -rn 'engines\|peerDependencies' package.json 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Not every CVE is exploitable in your usage (the vulnerable code path may be unused); audit output needs triage, and dev-only deps differ from runtime exposure
Updating must regenerate the lockfile and preserve integrity hashes; hand-editing versions without the lockfile yields non-reproducible or unverifiable installs
landmines.probe: ls package-lock.json yarn.lock pnpm-lock.yaml poetry.lock 2>/dev/null ; git diff --stat package-lock.json 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Each bump can change behavior; the suite must pass and the app must still build before committing, or you trade a CVE for an outage
landmines.probe: ls package.json && grep -n '"test"' package.json ; ls Makefile tox.ini 2>/dev/null

prompt: Rename our getUserData() function to fetchUserProfile() everywhere — it's used all over the codebase and it's also exported from our SDK.

landmines.desc: A rename must catch every call site across the repo including dynamic/string references, re-exports, and tests; a missed caller is a runtime break, so an exhaustive search precedes any edit
It's exported from the SDK, so renaming is a breaking public-API change for external consumers; needs a deprecation alias/major version bump, not a silent rename
A rename must be behavior-preserving; if the new name implies a different contract (Data vs Profile) callers may expect different semantics — verify the change is purely nominal
Compiled output, generated type declarations (.d.ts), and downstream typed consumers reference the old name; the build/types must regenerate and typecheck clean
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'getUserData' dist/ build/ types/ *.d.ts 2>/dev/null ; ls tsconfig.json 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Public docs, READMEs, and examples reference the old name and must update, plus a changelog entry for the breaking rename so consumers know to migrate

prompt: Implement a function that returns the median of a list of numbers. Wire it into our stats module.

landmines.desc: Median of an empty list is undefined and a naive mid-index access throws or returns garbage; the empty and single-element cases are the off-by-one traps that must be handled explicitly
For even-length input the median is the average of the two middle elements, not a single mid index; getting this wrong is an off-by-one that passes odd-length tests and fails even ones
landmines.probe: echo 'verify even-length branch averages the two middles'

landmines.desc: Sorting in place mutates the caller's list (a surprising side effect) and a non-copy sort changes the contract; also sorting is O(n log n) when a selection algorithm is O(n)
Averaging two large middle values as (a+b) can overflow fixed-width ints; use a-b/2 or wider type, and float medians need a defined precision/NaN handling
landmines.probe: echo 'check (a+b)/2 for overflow and NaN/inf inputs'

landmines.desc: NaN, None/null, strings, or unsortable mixed types corrupt the result silently; input validation defines the contract

prompt: Make the add-to-cart button feel instant by updating the cart count optimistically before the server responds. Implement the optimistic update.

landmines.desc: An optimistic update must roll back to the prior state if the server request fails, or the UI shows an item that wasn't actually added; the rollback path is the whole point and is the part that gets skipped
Rapid clicks fire overlapping requests; a slow earlier response can overwrite a newer one (last-write-wins race), so responses must be sequenced/cancelled or keyed to avoid a stale count
The optimistic value must be reconciled with the authoritative server count on success (the server may dedupe, cap quantity, or reject), not left as the guessed value
In SSR frameworks the optimistic/client state must match server-rendered HTML on first paint or React throws a hydration mismatch and the count flickers/resets
The count change is dynamic; without an aria-live region screen-reader users aren't notified, and the button needs disabled/busy state for keyboard users during the request

prompt: Set up the GitHub Actions workflow to publish our npm package automatically when we push a git tag. Wire up the publish step.

landmines.desc: The NPM_TOKEN must come from encrypted secrets and never be echoed; a 'set -x', a debug print, or logging the .npmrc leaks a publish token into world-readable Action logs
package.json version must match the pushed tag or you publish the wrong version (or npm rejects a duplicate); the workflow must verify they agree, not assume

Triggering publish on any pushed tag (or on pull_request from forks) lets an attacker or accidental tag publish arbitrary code with your token; the trigger must be locked to the right ref and protected
Publishing must build from a clean checkout with a locked dependency tree (npm ci, not npm install) so the published artifact is reproducible and not polluted by cached/local state
The 'files' allowlist / .npmignore decides what ships; a misconfig publishes source/secrets or omits the build output, and provenance/2FA settings affect whether publish even succeeds in CI
A shared/restorable CI cache keyed loosely can be poisoned to inject artifacts into the published package; cache keys must be scoped to lockfile hashes

prompt: Rename the users.email column to email_address in our app database. Write and run the migration.

landmines.desc: Renaming a live column instantly breaks every running app instance still querying the old name; a safe rename is an expand/contract (add new, dual-write, backfill, switch reads, drop old), not a single ALTER
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'email\b' src/ models/ --include='*.{js,ts,py,go,sql}' | grep -iv email_address | head -30

landmines.desc: On large tables a rename/rewrite can take a heavy lock blocking reads/writes; verify the engine does the rename as metadata-only or schedule for a maintenance path
landmines.probe: echo 'check table size and whether the DB does rename in-place'; grep -rn 'reltuples\|COUNT' migrations/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Views, indexes, foreign keys, triggers, and downstream ETL/replication referencing users.email must be updated together or they silently break after the rename
landmines.probe: echo 'list views/triggers/indexes referencing the column'; grep -rln 'users.email\|\.email' dbt/ etl/ views/ 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: The migration needs a tested down/rollback; a failed deploy mid-rename with no reverse leaves the schema and app out of sync with no clean recovery
Logical replication / CDC consumers (Debezium, read replicas) keyed on the column name can stall or drop rows when the schema changes underneath them
landmines.probe: grep -rn 'replication\|debezium\|cdc\|slot\|publication' . 2>/dev/null ; echo 'check for active logical replication on this table'

prompt: Our checkout calls the inventory service then the payment service then the shipping service. Add the orchestration so an order goes through all three. Wire the calls.

landmines.desc: If payment succeeds but shipping fails (or any middle step), the system is left half-committed; a distributed flow needs compensating actions / saga rollback, not a naive sequential call chain that strands money or inventory
Network timeouts make it unknown whether a call succeeded; retries must carry idempotency keys per step or a retry double-reserves inventory or double-charges
Inventory must be reserved (not just checked) before payment, or two concurrent orders both pass the check and oversell; the check-then-act across services is a distributed TOCTOU
A downstream service hanging without a timeout cascades into checkout hanging; per-call timeouts and a circuit breaker bound the partial-failure blast radius
There's no exactly-once across services; the design must be at-least-once + idempotent or at-most-once + reconciliation, and pretending the happy-path chain is exactly-once hides the failure modes
Crashed mid-saga orders need a reconciliation/sweeper to detect and resolve stuck states; without it orders silently rot in a half-done status

prompt: Rename the local helper formatName to formatFullName in utils.js — it's only used inside that one file. Update the two call sites in the same file.
There's a typo on our payment form — the button says 'Procede to Payment'. Fix it to 'Proceed to Payment'.
Add a one-line comment above the awaitWithRetry async helper explaining what it does. No logic changes.
Run the formatter (black) on transform.py to fix the indentation, then show me the diff. Don't change any logic.
In train_test.py rename the variable acc to accuracy_value for readability. It's a local in one test function.
Add a docstring to the existing median() function describing its parameters and return value. Don't touch the implementation.
In CONTRIBUTING.md fix the typo where 'refactor' is spelled 'refacter'. One word.
Add a comment above the webhook route in routes.js noting which events it handles. Don't change the handler.
Rename the CSS class .cart-btn-v2 to .cart-button in styles.css and the one component that uses it. Cosmetic only.
Add a comment in the release workflow yaml noting that it runs on tag pushes. Don't change any steps or triggers.
Our nightly batch job keeps getting OOM-killed as the dataset grows. Bump the memory limit and load the whole file into a list to speed it up.

landmines.desc: OOM that scales with dataset size points to unbounded memory growth; you must measure where allocation happens (heap profile / memory tracker) before raising limits or rewriting, or you just delay the same crash
landmines.probe: echo 'run a memory profiler (tracemalloc / memray / heap snapshot) on a sample run'; grep -rn 'memory\|RSS\|tracemalloc\|memray' . 2>/dev/null

landmines.desc: Loading the whole growing file into a list is O(n) memory and is the opposite of the fix; the scalable approach streams/chunks the input so memory stays O(1) in dataset size
A leak often comes from accumulating results/caches/closures that are never released across iterations; bumping the limit hides an unbounded structure that needs to be flushed periodically
Raising the memory limit treats the symptom; if growth is unbounded the job will OOM again at the next data-size tier, so the limit bump is a Dumb fix that defers the real work
If results are written at the end, buffering everything before a single write forces holding it all in memory; writing in batches bounds memory and also avoids one giant transaction
